articles.title: Early summer scorches Gujarat as mercury rises above 40°C: Heat breaks 3-year record; Heatwave alert in Ahm... - Bhaskar English
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Gujarat
articles.full_text:
Severe heatwave warning across Gujarat as mercury may touch 45°C:100-yr record broken in Diu; red alert for Rajkot-Surendranagar, orange alert in 10 districts
- Copy link
The scorching heat has taken on a fierce form, where in just the first 10 days of March, the temperature has crossed 40 degrees in the state, breaking all records of the last three years. The Meteorological Department has predicted severe heatwave in the state till March 16, declaring 'Orange Alert' in 6 districts including Morbi, Rajkot and Kutch, while 'Yellow Alert' has been issued in 12 districts including Ahmedabad, Gandhinagar and Surat.
Due to the heat dome effect and hot dry winds from the northwest, the temperature in Ahmedabad has crossed 41 degrees. According to weather experts, as temperatures are likely to reach 43 to 45 degrees in the next 3 to 4 days, citizens have been appealed to remain cautious during afternoon hours.
Orange alert issued for severe heatwave in several districts
According to Weather Department Director Dr. A.K. Das, “Heatwave conditions may develop in the state until March 16. The Weather Department has issued an orange alert for severe heatwave in several districts for today, (March 11)".
He added, "The department has issued orange alerts for heatwave in Kutch, Banaskantha, Patan, Surendranagar, Morbi, and Rajkot. While yellow alerts for heatwave have been issued for Sabarkantha, Mehsana, Gandhinagar, Ahmedabad, Botad, Bhavnagar, Amreli, Gir Somnath, Junagadh, Jamnagar, Vadodara and Surat”.
Wind will blow from northwest direction at speed of 15-20 km per hour
Humid weather conditions even at night, are predicted in Ahmedabad, Gandhinagar, Banaskantha, Anand, Patan, Kutch, Surendranagar and Diu districts. Coastal areas are likely to experience hot and humid weather until March 13 and winds will blow from the northwest direction at a speed of 15 to 20 kilometers per hour.
Heatwave forecast in these 8 districts on March 12
According to the weather department's forecast, there will be no significant change in temperature for the next two days. After that, there is a possibility of temperature dropping by about 2 to 4 degrees. The sky is likely to remain clear in Ahmedabad city today. The maximum temperature is likely to be around 41 degrees Celsius and the minimum temperature around 23 degrees. While tomorrow, on March 12, a heatwave warning with yellow alert has been issued for Banaskantha, Patan, Mehsana, Kutch, Surendranagar, Morbi, Rajkot and Amreli districts.
Temperature likely to reach 43 degrees in 3-4 days
Weather expert A.T. Desai said that currently the humidity level in the atmosphere has dropped to 10-20%, which normally remains at 50-60%. This causes skin-burning heat sensation. Looking at these conditions, there is a high possibility of temperature reaching 43 degrees in the next 3-4 days. Looking at the data of the last four years at the beginning of March in Ahmedabad, the heat in 2026 has broken all records.
Surprising temperature spike in first 10 days
In Ahmedabad, the temperature is continuously rising this year. According to available information, there has been a surprising spike in temperature in just the first 10 days of March. The temperature which was 34.2C on March 1, has increased to 39.4C by March 8. Compared to 2024 and 2025, the heat levels recorded in 2026 are much higher.
However, according to estimates, there is a strong possibility that the temperature will exceed 40 degrees Celsius and 41 degrees Celsius in the coming days in many parts of the state.
Municipal hospitals will reserve 10 beds, guidelines for people
It's dangerous when temperature doesn't drop at night after intense heat during day
It is considered a dangerous situation when the temperature doesn't decrease at night after intense heat during the day. This has happened in previous years.
Heat started being felt at night from March
When high pressure forms in the atmosphere, hot winds from the lower levels come directly towards the ground. Due to this process, when the sky remains clear, sunrays falling directly on the ground suddenly raise the temperature.
Concrete, roads and large buildings absorb heat, causing cities to experience more heat than surrounding rural areas. With a 5.2 degree increase in minimum temperature, heat started being felt at night from March.
Like every year, Municipality will provide ORS water under Heat Action Plan
Following the orange alert, the Municipality of Ahmedabad has announced its old heat action plan. 350 water points have been set up in the city. They will provide ORS at 1000 locations. Last year, about 20 lakh ORS packets were distributed.
Temperature increased due to these 4 reasons
What is Loo or Heatwave?
According to IMD (India Meteorological Department), when the maximum temperature reaches 40 degrees Celsius in plains and 30 degrees Celsius in hilly areas, hot winds start blowing. If the temperature in any area reaches 47 degrees Celsius, it is considered a dangerous category of heatwave.
What causes heat stroke?
Our body's normal temperature is 37 degrees Celsius. However, during summer, the surrounding temperature reaches up to 40 to 45 degrees. In such conditions, when we go out in intense sunlight or hot air, the surrounding environment increases our body temperature. When our body temperature rises above the normal body temperature of 37 degrees Celsius, this condition is called heat stroke.
What kind of home remedies can be used in cases of heat stroke?
In cases of heat stroke, the first priority is to control the increased body temperature and eliminate dehydration. In such situations, these remedies can be adopted:
If a person is suffering from mental problems such as stuttering, fainting or mental tremors, this is a condition of dangerous level heat stroke. In such a situation, the patient should be taken to the hospital immediately as there is a risk of damage to body organs in this. The person should not treat themselves in any way. Because in such a situation, even a little carelessness can cause the death of the patient.
What precautions should be taken to avoid heat stroke?
If you experience any kind of problem, contact a doctor immediately.
What should be the diet to prevent heat stroke?
According to medical experts, eat something before going out to prevent heat stroke. Grandparents say that one should never leave home on an empty stomach, there is science behind it. When you go out on an empty stomach, you get tired quickly and lose electrolytes, which causes dehydration. You can drink coconut water to prevent heat and sunstroke.
Additionally, you should not drink cold water immediately after coming from sunlight or after excessive sweating. In summer, you should drink water mixed with lemon and salt two-three times a day. This reduces the risk of heat stroke.
For those who are troubled by heat stroke, make a paste by grinding barley flour and onion and apply it on the body, it will give them a lot of relief. After coming out from the sun, licking some onion juice mixed with honey reduces the risk of heat stroke.
Eating jaggery after meals in summer season also reduces the risk of heat stroke. In summer, one should take light food and include curd in the diet.

articles.title: Blistering heat wave bakes Gujarat, Rajkot sizzles at 42°C - The Times of India
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Gujarat
articles.full_text:
Rajkot: Intense heat baked large parts of Gujarat on Tuesday, with temperatures that rose well above normal leaving people struggling against the early onset of the summer.
Rajkot recorded the highest maximum temperature in Saurashtra at 42°C, nearly 7.7 degrees above normal, while Ahmedabad sizzled at 41.4°C, about 7.2 degrees above normal. Bhuj recorded 41°C, while Amreli touched 40.3°C. Diu, the Union territory and a favourite getaway for Gujaratis, recorded temperatures as much as 10 degrees above normal.
Several districts have crossed the 40°C mark in the first week of March, pointing to what could be a long and harsh summer.
The scorching afternoon heat kept many people indoors, leaving roads and markets unusually quiet in the afternoon as residents avoided stepping out.
According to the India meteorological department (IMD), discomfort due to hot and humid conditions is very likely to prevail over coastal areas of the Gujarat region and Saurashtra-Kutch in the next couple days.
Across cities, roadside vendors selling sugarcane juice, buttermilk and tender coconuts did brisk business. Construction workers and delivery personnel covered their faces with scarves and cloth to shield themselves from the hot wind.
Weather officials said heatwave to severe heat wave conditions prevailed at many places in Saurashtra-Kutch, while a few pockets of the Gujarat region also experienced intense heat.
Rajkot, Surendranagar, Keshod, Bhavnagar, Porbandar and Kutch districts recorded heatwave conditions. In the Gujarat region, pockets of Ahmedabad, Gandhinagar, Deesa, Surat and Vadodara also experienced sweltering conditions.

articles.title: Heatwave in Gujarat: Blistering 41.4°C keeps Ahmedabad in heat grip, yellow alert on - The Times of India
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Gujarat
articles.full_text:
Ahmedabad: The city is currently locked in a hot embrace with summer, recording a blistering 41.4°C for the second day straight — a staggering 7.2 degrees above the seasonal normal. While Rajkot claimed the state's "hot seat" at 42°C, the entire region is feeling the singe.
Even night brings little relief as the city recorded a minimum temperature of 23.1°C, five degrees higher than usual.
The heatwave, which began right at the summer season's start, will continue to torment Gujarat on Wednesday. Though temperatures are likely to ease from Thursday.
This early-season surge has caught many off guard, as the transition from a mild February to a scorching March happened with brutal speed.
In Ahmedabad, the relentless "heat island" effect caused by the concrete corridors has amplified the discomfort, making the 41.4°C peak feel significantly more intense for those commuting. "It is just the beginning of March and the heat is already unbearable. I avoid going out in the afternoon hours. I scheduled all my meetings either early in the morning or late in the evening. I have also advised my employees to use umbrellas and caps when going out," said Chirag Patel, a businessman from Vastrapur.
Ahmedabad Municipal Corporation (AMC) issued a heat action plan (HAP) on Tuesday, urging citizens to stay hydrated, wear light clothes, use caps and umbrellas when possible, and consume ORS. Phsyicians in the city are also advising a "less is more" approach to diet, swapping spicy meals for lime water and seasonal fruits like watermelon to navigate this early-season torment.
The India meteorological department (IMD) issued orange alert issued for 8 districts and yellow alert for 13 others, including Ahmedabad and Gandhinagar, on Wednesday. It predicted: "Severe heatwave conditions very likely to prevail at a few pockets in Banaskantha and Patan; at many pockets in Surendranagar, Rajkot Morbi and Kutch." Meanwhile, the hot and humid conditions also prevailed in the coastal regions of the state. Warm night conditions are likely to prevail in parts of north and Central Gujarat districts on Wednesday.

articles.title: Ahmedabad heatwave squeezes wallets: Lemon prices jump from ₹80 to ₹250 in 10 days - Ahmedabad Mirror
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Gujarat
articles.full_text:
Ahmedabad Civic
Ahmedabad heatwave squeezes wallets: Lemon prices jump from ₹80 to ₹250 in 10 days
Ahmedabad: Lemon prices in Ahmedabad have sharply increased at the start of summer, touching ₹200–₹250 per kg in the retail market. The price, which was around ₹60–₹80 per kg earlier, has increased nearly four times within just 10 days, creating concern among consumers.
Ahmedabad: The sudden rise comes as temperatures across Gujarat, including Ahmedabad, have crossed 41°C in early March. With the heat increasing, demand for drinks like lemonade, shikanji, lemon soda and sugarcane juice has also surged, pushing up lemon prices much earlier than usual.
Traders say lemon prices usually rise in April and May, but this year the spike has already appeared in March. In the wholesale market at Jamalpur APMC, lemons are currently priced between ₹90 and ₹120 per kg, while retail prices have reached around ₹250 per kg.
Interestingly, the price rise has come despite an increase in supply. Lemon arrivals at Jamalpur yard during 2025–26 have increased by about 20 percent compared to the previous year, reaching around 1.75 lakh quintals, which is 31,000 quintals more than 2024–25.
Market experts believe that along with demand, the growing influence of profiteers and arbitrary pricing in the market has also contributed to the sudden hike.
About half of the lemons sold in Ahmedabad come from Saurashtra regions such as Bhavnagar and Palitana, while the rest are supplied from southern states including Tamil Nadu, Hyderabad and Maharashtra. Traders also say that the supply of the popular Mehsana lemon variety has reduced this season.
Currently, nearly 50 percent fresh lemons and 50 percent cold-storage stock are being sold in the market. Due to the high prices, many people are now choosing flavoured energy drinks instead of lemon water during the heat.
Caption: Despite higher arrivals of lemons in the market this year, prices in Ahmedabad have surged to ₹250 per kg due to rising demand and limited fresh supply.

articles.title: Gujarat Reels Under Intense Heat, Maximum Temperature Goes as High as 42°C - DeshGujarat
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Gujarat
articles.full_text:
Gujarat Reels Under Intense Heat, Maximum Temperature Goes as High as 42°C
March 11, 2026
Rajkot: Gujarat witnessed intense heat on Tuesday, with maximum temperatures soaring well above normal across most parts of the state, with Rajkot emerging as the hottest city at 42.0°C, according to the weather report issued by the India Meteorological Department (IMD) this morning.
Ahmedabad recorded a maximum temperature of 41.4°C, which was 7.2 degrees above normal, indicating unusually high heat for early March. The minimum temperature in the city was also above normal at 22.5°C, showing a rise of 4.6 degrees, while humidity dropped to as low as 16% in the evening, increasing discomfort due to dry heat.
Several other cities also reported temperatures above 40°C. Baroda touched 41.0°C, Bhuj 41.0°C, Deesa 41.0°C, Porbandar 40.9°C, Gandhinagar 40.8°C, Veraval 40.8°C, Surat 40.2°C, and Amreli 40.3°C. In many of these locations, temperatures were 5 to 9 degrees above normal, indicating widespread heat conditions across the state.
Rajkot recorded the hottest day among major cities, with the maximum temperature 8 degrees above normal, while the neighbouring Union Territory of Diu showed the largest departure, with 10 degrees above normal, reaching 41.3°C.
Coastal locations also experienced unusual heat. Bhavnagar recorded 39.6°C, Kandla 39.5°C, and Veraval 40.8°C, all significantly above seasonal averages. Even hill and tribal regions such as Dang reported temperatures close to 39°C, suggesting that the heat is affecting nearly the entire state.
Weather officials indicated that temperatures are currently running well above normal for March, and such conditions may continue over the next few days if no weather system develops over the region. Furthermore, a heatwave warning has been issued till tomorrow in parts of the state. DeshGujarat

articles.title: Ahmedabad Heatwave Risk Prompts School Timing Debate - urbanacres.in
articles.search_term: heatwave (Ahmedabad OR Amreli OR Anand OR Aravalli OR "Banaskantha (Palanpur)" OR Bharuch OR Bhavnagar OR Botad OR "Chhota Udepur" OR Dahod OR "Dangs (Ahwa)" OR "Devbhoomi Dwarka" OR Gandhinagar OR "Gir Somnath" OR Jamnagar OR Junagadh OR Kachchh OR "Kheda (Nadiad)" OR Mahisagar OR Mehsana OR Morbi OR "Narmada (Rajpipla)" OR Navsari OR "Panchmahal (Godhra)" OR Patan OR Porbandar OR Rajkot OR "Sabarkantha (Himmatnagar)" OR Surat OR Surendranagar OR "Tapi (Vyara)" OR Vadodara OR Valsad)
articles.full_text:
Rising temperatures across Gujarat have triggered renewed calls to adjust school schedules, with education groups urging authorities to introduce Gujarat school morning timings to reduce heat exposure for students. The proposal comes as several districts report temperatures climbing above 40°C, raising concerns about student safety during afternoon travel and classroom hours. Representatives from a statewide teachers’ and education organisation have formally approached the state’s education department, recommending that schools temporarily operate during early morning hours until the onset of the annual summer vacation. The suggestion covers government and grant-in-aid institutions across primary, secondary and higher secondary levels.
Weather data in recent days indicates a rapid increase in daytime temperatures across much of the state. Meteorological agencies have also indicated the possibility of heatwave-like conditions in parts of western India during the coming weeks, a trend that has become more frequent in recent summers.
Education experts say the proposal for Gujarat school morning timings reflects growing concern about how extreme weather affects daily life in rapidly urbanising regions. In cities such as Ahmedabad, school hours often coincide with peak heat periods, particularly when afternoon dispersal occurs during the hottest part of the day. Students travelling on foot, by bicycle or using public transport can face prolonged exposure to high temperatures. Younger children are especially vulnerable to dehydration, fatigue and heat-related health risks during extended outdoor travel.
Urban health professionals note that rising temperatures are increasingly affecting routine urban services, including school operations, construction work and public transport schedules. As cities expand and built-up areas intensify, heat retention in dense neighbourhoods often amplifies the impact of extreme weather.
Supporters of Gujarat school morning timings argue that adjusting schedules could significantly reduce student exposure to high afternoon temperatures without disrupting the academic calendar. Morning sessions, they say, would allow schools to complete instructional hours before peak heat levels set in later in the day. Similar scheduling changes have been implemented in several Indian states during periods of intense summer heat. Education administrators often view such adjustments as a short-term adaptation measure while broader climate resilience strategies are developed.
Urban planners suggest that the issue also highlights the importance of climate-sensitive infrastructure in educational facilities. Schools designed with better ventilation, shaded playgrounds and climate-responsive architecture can help reduce heat stress for students and staff. The debate over Gujarat school morning timings also reflects a broader challenge facing Indian cities as climate patterns shift. Heatwaves are becoming more frequent and longer-lasting, forcing policymakers to rethink how daily schedules, urban design and public services adapt to rising temperatures. State education authorities are expected to review the proposal in consultation with district administrators and weather officials. If implemented, revised school hours could remain in place until the beginning of the summer break, when academic institutions traditionally close for several weeks. For families and educators across Gujarat, the discussion underscores how climate realities are increasingly shaping decisions that affect everyday life in cities. Managing rising temperatures may require not only short-term measures like schedule changes, but also long-term investments in climate-resilient urban infrastructure and planning.
Ahmedabad Heatwave Risk Prompts School Timing Debate
